summary:
The patient should undergo an eye exam due to their recent onset of type 1 diabetes; routine yearly checks are recommended by medical guidelines starting immediately post-diagnosis despite asymptomatic status at present.
The recommendation is based primarily on preventing potential vision loss associated with diabetic retinopathy through timely intervention. To ensure comprehensive care, additional assessments such as foot evaluations may also be considered given the broader implications of long-term diabetes management but were not directly addressed here focusing solely on ocular health concerns raised initially. Given these considerations,
To summarize:

A person newly diagnosed with Type I Diabetes within less than two years must receive prompt ophthalmologic evaluation according to established healthcare protocols aimed at detecting signs indicative of progressive microvascular damage like diabetic retinopathies which can lead irreversible blindness if left untreated even without apparent visual impairment currently observed. Annual follow-ups remain crucial irrespective of initial symptomatology ensuring optimal outcomes via vigilant surveillance strategies endorsed universally across professional bodies involved in managing chronic metabolic disorders including endocrinologists alongside optometrists/ophthalmologist teams working collaboratively towards holistic disease control plans tailored specifically per individual needs while prioritizing preventive measures over reactive treatments whenever possible under current best practices framework governing modern medicine approaches toward treating complex To ensure comprehensive care, additional assessments such as foot evaluations may also be considered given the broader implications of long-term diabetes management but were not directly addressed here focusing solely on ocular health concerns raised initially.
